title: Politics and regional interests
description: Test your skills on how different regional interests affected debates about the role of the federal government in the early republic.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“It eventuates in this: whether you, as a planter will consent to be taxed, in order to hire another man to go to work in a shoemaker’s shop, or to set up a spinning jenny. For my part I will not agree to it, even though they should, by way of return, agree to be taxed to help us to plant tobacco; much less will I agree to pay all, and receive nothing for it. No, I will buy where I can get manufactures cheapest; I will not agree to lay a duty on the cultivators of the soil to encourage exotic manufactures. . .” 

>-Souce: John Randolph, Opposition to the Tariff of 1816, [The Debates and Proceedings in the Congress of the United States: With an Appendix, Containing Important State Papers and Public Documents, and All the Laws of a Public Nature; with a Copious Index, Volume 29](https://books.google.com/books?id=X61CAQAAMAAJ&printsec=frontcover&source=gbs_ge_summary_r&cad=0), 1816

**Which of the following statements best summarizes Randolph’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He opposes the tax on imports because it only benefits Northern industry and not the Southern economy.
He supports the tax on imports because it will help fund internal improvements in both the North and the South.
He opposes the tax on imports because it only benefits the Southern agriculture and not Northern industry.
He supports the tax on imports because it will help fund new technological improvements to agricultural machines.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“It eventuates in this: whether you, as a planter will consent to be taxed, in order to hire another man to go to work in a shoemaker’s shop, or to set up a spinning jenny. For my part I will not agree to it, even though they should, by way of return, agree to be taxed to help us to plant tobacco; much less will I agree to pay all, and receive nothing for it. No, I will buy where I can get manufactures cheapest; I will not agree to lay a duty on the cultivators of the soil to encourage exotic manufactures. . .” 

>-Source: John Randolph, Opposition to the Tariff of 1816, [The Debates and Proceedings in the Congress of the United States: With an Appendix, Containing Important State Papers and Public Documents, and All the Laws of a Public Nature; with a Copious Index, Volume 29](https://books.google.com/books?id=X61CAQAAMAAJ&printsec=frontcover&source=gbs_ge_summary_r&cad=0), 1816

**The excerpt best serves as evidence for which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: increasing regional differences over economic policy
increasing regional differences about the extension of slavery
decreasing efforts by the United States to engage in international trade
declining support for the Democratic Party in the South

assessment_items.question_content:
>“It eventuates in this: whether you, as a planter will consent to be taxed, in order to hire another man to go to work in a shoemaker’s shop, or to set up a spinning jenny. For my part I will not agree to it, even though they should, by way of return, agree to be taxed to help us to plant tobacco; much less will I agree to pay all, and receive nothing for it. No, I will buy where I can get manufactures cheapest; I will not agree to lay a duty on the cultivators of the soil to encourage exotic manufactures. . .” 

>-Souce: John Randolph, Opposition to the Tariff of 1816, [The Debates and Proceedings in the Congress of the United States: With an Appendix, Containing Important State Papers and Public Documents, and All the Laws of a Public Nature; with a Copious Index, Volume 29](https://books.google.com/books?id=X61CAQAAMAAJ&printsec=frontcover&source=gbs_ge_summary_r&cad=0), 1816

**The excerpt most directly expresses an economic perspective that**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: prioritized regional interests.
encouraged federal funding for internal improvements.
sought to protect United States foreign trade.
supported the new tariffs on exports.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “In the meantime, what has agriculture been doing in spite of all Mr. Clay’s efforts to convert our young farmers into manufacturers? . . . Our agriculture is spreading in every direction, not only counties but by States, while population in our manufacturing regions is almost stationary. . . Although agriculture must thus outgrow this legislative home market, till our unexplored forests on north-western, western, and south-western borders, are converted into fields and pastures, we must go on taxing ourselves for generations to come, to increase the wealth of a small portion of our wealthy men and their posterity. Strip this American system of all its sophistries, and what is it, but a fraudulent partnership between a portion of our politicians and capitalists . . .”

>-Source: “Commercial Reciprocity and the American System,” *The United States Democratic Review,* 1844

**Based on the text, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The American System benefitted industrial economies more than agricultural economies.
The federal government had the power to tax imports and exports under the necessary and proper clause.
The American System would help to connect and unify all the different regions of the United States.
The Southern states should diversify their agricultural products to improve their local economy.

assessment_items.question_content:
“In the meantime, what has agriculture been doing in spite of all Mr. Clay’s efforts to convert our young farmers into manufacturers? . . . Our agriculture is spreading in every direction, not only counties but by States, while population in our manufacturing regions is almost stationary. . . Although agriculture must thus outgrow this legislative home market, till our unexplored forests on north-western, western, and south-western borders, are converted into fields and pastures, we must go on taxing ourselves for generations to come, to increase the wealth of a small portion of our wealthy men and their posterity. Strip this American system of all its sophistries, and what is it, but a fraudulent partnership between a portion of our politicians and capitalists . . .”


>-Source: “Commercial Reciprocity and the American System,” *The United States Democratic Review,* 1844

**Which of the following groups would be most likely to support the views expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: plantation owners in the South
merchants in the Northeast
railroad workers in the West
factory owners in the Northeast

assessment_items.question_content:
>“In the meantime, what has agriculture been doing in spite of all Mr. Clay’s efforts to convert our young farmers into manufacturers? . . . Our agriculture is spreading in every direction, not only counties but by States, while population in our manufacturing regions is almost stationary. . . Although agriculture must thus outgrow this legislative home market, till our unexplored forests on north-western, western, and south-western borders, are converted into fields and pastures, we must go on taxing ourselves for generations to come, to increase the wealth of a small portion of our wealthy men and their posterity. Strip this American system of all its sophistries, and what is it, but a fraudulent partnership between a portion of our politicians and capitalists . . .”

>-Source: “Commercial Reciprocity and the American System,” *The United States Democratic Review,* 1844

**The excerpt best reflects which of the following developments from 1820 to 1850?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: alignment of political parties based on regional issues
efforts by the United States to increase international trade
collapse of the Whig Party in the North
adoption of a new policy in the South to diversify economic production

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Federal funding for infrastructure improvements, 1820-1840** ||
| 1820 | 1830 | 1840
- | - | - | -
Northeast  | \$0.8 million | \$6.0 million | \$8.4 million
West | N/A | \$1.0 million | \$4.7 million
South | \$0.3 million | \$0.5 million | \$1.2 million

>-Source: Bureau of the Census, *Historical Statistics of the United States, Colonial Times to 1970*

**Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the data in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Northeast received more funds for internal improvements than the South.
The South had more representation in the federal government.
The Northeast and West earned more profit from exported goods than the South.
The South received more funds for railroads and canals than the West.

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Federal funding for infrastructure improvements, 1820-1840** ||
| 1820 | 1830 | 1840
- | - | - | -
Northeast  | \$0.8 million | \$6.0 million | \$8.4 million
West | N/A | \$1.0 million | \$4.7 million
South | \$0.3 million | \$0.5 million | \$1.2 million

>-Source: Bureau of the Census, *Historical Statistics of the United States, Colonial Times to 1970*

**Which of the following was a direct effect of the trend in funding for internal improvements after 1840 shown on the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The North and West became more connected.
The South outpaced the Northeast in factory production of goods.
The Northeast transitioned to an agricultural economy.
The South created stronger connections to international trading partners.

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Federal funding for infrastructure improvements, 1820-1840** ||
| 1820 | 1830 | 1840
- | - | - | -
Northeast  | \$0.8 million | \$6.0 million | \$8.4 million
West | N/A | \$1.0 million | \$4.7 million
South | \$0.3 million | \$0.5 million | \$1.2 million

>-Source: Bureau of the Census, *Historical Statistics of the United States, Colonial Times to 1970*

**The main trend shown in the table was most directly associated with which of the following government policies occurring in the United States at the time?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The American System
The Monroe Doctrine
The Missouri Compromise
The Louisiana Purchase

assessment_items.question_content:
> “[T]his momentous question, like a firebell in the night, awakened and filled me with terror. I considered it, at once as the [death] knell of the Union. It is hushed, indeed, for the moment. But this is a reprieve only, not a final sentence. A geographical line, coinciding with a marked principle, moral and political, once conceived, and held up to the angry passions of men, will never be obliterated; and every new irritation will mark it deeper and deeper.”

>-Thomas Jefferson, in a letter to John Randolph, 1820

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical events?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Missouri Compromise
The Nullification Crisis
The Louisiana Purchase
The Monroe Doctrine

assessment_items.question_content:
> “[T]his momentous question, like a firebell in the night, awakened and filled me with terror. I considered it, at once as the [death] knell of the Union. It is hushed, indeed, for the moment. But this is a reprieve only, not a final sentence. A geographical line, coinciding with a marked principle, moral and political, once conceived, and held up to the angry passions of men, will never be obliterated; and every new irritation will mark it deeper and deeper.”

>-Thomas Jefferson, in a letter to John Randolph, 1820

**Which of the following developments in the 1840s best represented the continuation of the ideas expressed in the passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: debates over the expansion of slavery to land acquired after the Mexican-American War
conflicts with European powers over continued colonization efforts in the Americas
disputes over the federal government’s role in influencing the economy
clashes between citizens and German and Irish immigrants over increased immigration to the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
> “[T]his momentous question, like a firebell in the night, awakened and filled me with terror. I considered it, at once as the [death] knell of the Union. It is hushed, indeed, for the moment. But this is a reprieve only, not a final sentence. A geographical line, coinciding with a marked principle, moral and political, once conceived, and held up to the angry passions of men, will never be obliterated; and every new irritation will mark it deeper and deeper.”

>-Thomas Jefferson, in a letter to John Randolph, 1820

**Jefferson’s remarks in the excerpt most directly reflected which of the following developments during the early nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: increasing regional differences over the expansion of slavery
decreasing regional differences in economic practices
growing  support for the end of trade relationships with Europe and Asia
declining use of the Atlantic Slave Trade in the United States
